Scalability (can grow from 50→5000-perso: Low — central controller is single point of failure; scaling requires controller replacement
Regulatory Burden (Zone 5): Moderate — standard IEEE 1547 interconnection; full PUC approval required; 4–12 month timeline
Zone 5 Suitability (climate + infrastruc: High — uses standard AC appliances and electrician skills; compatible with all Zone 5 utility territories
Resilience (120h winter outage): Moderate — requires 400–600 kWh storage per 100 kW generation for 10-hour overnight gap; generator backup required for 120h winter
Storage Overhead (kWh storage required p: 4–6 kWh/kW for summer; 8–12 kWh/kW for Zone 5 winter 120h
Key Limitation: Single point of failure; 10–15% conversion losses; centralized EMS failure disables entire microgrid
Vendors / Resources: Schneider Electric EcoStruxure; Eaton Microgrid; muGrid Analytics (Bayfield County); Scale Microgrids R2M2

Scalability (can grow from 50→5000-perso: Low — limited off-the-shelf appliance compatibility; requires full redesign of building wiring
Regulatory Burden (Zone 5): High — fewer certified contractors; limited state PUC precedent for DC distribution; insurance/code issues
Zone 5 Suitability (climate + infrastruc: Moderate — native to solar/battery but most Zone 5 farms and homes are AC-wired; motor loads (pumps) require DC motors or inverter
Resilience (120h winter outage): High — 3–5% conversion losses vs 15%+ for AC; best for new-construction communities with LED + EV + electronic loads
Storage Overhead (kWh storage required p: 3–5 kWh/kW for summer; 6–10 kWh/kW for Zone 5 winter 120h
Key Limitation: Incompatible with existing AC appliances and motor loads; requires entire community to be built to DC standards; very limited installer pool
Vendors / Resources: Victron Energy (48V DC systems); Meanwell (DC power supplies); IEEE 2030.10-2021 standard; ETH Zurich DC microgrid research

Scalability (can grow from 50→5000-perso: High — modular architecture; add DC bus loads over time; EMS can coordinate multiple sites
Regulatory Burden (Zone 5): Moderate — standard IEEE 1547 with additional bidirectional inverter documentation; 6–14 month timeline with IOU; 4–7 months with cooperative REC
Zone 5 Suitability (climate + infrastruc: Very High — 5–8% total conversion losses; works with existing AC appliances; solar and battery natively DC; validated at Bayfield County and UC San Diego
Resilience (120h winter outage): High — 50ms island transfer; GFM inverters handle frequency without generator; generator supplements for 120h winter; best overall Zone 5 performance
Storage Overhead (kWh storage required p: 3–5 kWh/kW for summer; 5–8 kWh/kW for Zone 5 winter 120h (with generator supplement)
Key Limitation: More complex commissioning than pure AC; requires GFM-capable inverters (not all residential inverters qualify); higher upfront design cost
Vendors / Resources: muGrid Analytics; SMA Sunny Island + Sunny Boy Storage; Schneider Electric XW+; Victron Quattro; Enphase IQ8 (household scale)

Capital Cost per kW (installed): "$3,000–$5,500/kW (per cluster); lower incremental cost for additional clusters"
Scalability (can grow from 50→5000-perso: Very High — each cluster operates independently; add clusters as community grows; ORNL multi-microgrid orchestrator model
Regulatory Burden (Zone 5): High — each cluster requires separate interconnection application; multi-site coordination may trigger MISO-level review for large aggregations
Zone 5 Suitability (climate + infrastruc: High — fault isolation between clusters; Zone 5 winter storm can knock out one cluster without affecting others; resilient against physical infrastructure damage
Resilience (120h winter outage): Very High — each cluster self-sustaining; shared generation between clusters via ORNL orchestrator model increases effective resilience; no single failure point
Storage Overhead (kWh storage required p: 4–7 kWh/kW per cluster (can be lower if clusters share generation via communication link)
Key Limitation: Most expensive and complex to design and commission; requires communication infrastructure linking clusters; highest regulatory complexity; 30–48 month timeline
Vendors / Resources: ORNL multi-microgrid orchestrator (Vieques PR model); OpenFMB peer-to-peer protocol; muGrid Analytics; NRECA CARED model

Scalability (can grow from 50→5000-perso: Very High — fully decentralized; no single controller; each node adds autonomously
Regulatory Burden (Zone 5): Very High — no established regulatory framework for P2P microgrids in Zone 5 states; FERC Order 2222 P2P provisions still in rulemaking
Zone 5 Suitability (climate + infrastruc: Low–Moderate — technology is research/early-commercial stage; few Zone 5 installations; installer expertise scarce; not yet appropriate for communities without engineering staff
Resilience (120h winter outage): High in theory — no single point of failure; nodes negotiate locally; but commissioning complexity creates practical reliability risk if operators lack deep expertise
Storage Overhead (kWh storage required p: 4–8 kWh/kW (optimization depends on P2P algorithm quality)
Key Limitation: Emerging technology — no Zone 5 community deployments documented as of May 2026; regulatory framework incomplete; commissioning requires power systems PhD-level expertise or equivalent; high installation failure risk without specialized vendor
Vendors / Resources: pymgrid (open-source simulation); OpenFMB P2P extensions; arXiv P2P energy trading research; blockchain energy trading (research stage)

Scalability (can grow from 50→5000-perso: Very High — scales to 5000-person and beyond; FERC Order 2222 wholesale market participation
Regulatory Burden (Zone 5): Very High — MISO Order 2222 compliance proceedings ongoing May 2026; requires legal counsel for market participation; complex aggregation rules; 3–5 year timeline
Zone 5 Suitability (climate + infrastruc: Moderate — best fit for Zone 5 communities near MISO infrastructure; Iowa and Illinois have most favorable renewable context; standalone islanding capability requires additional investment beyond VPP participation
Resilience (120h winter outage): Moderate — optimized for grid-connected revenue generation; islanding capability requires additional $15
Best Fit Community Size: 000 per site beyond VPP infrastructure; 120h island capability not guaranteed without dedicated storage per site
Key Limitation: 2–4 kWh/kW for grid-connected operation; 6–10 kWh/kW if 120h islanding capability is added
column12: Not viable at 50–500-person scale; MISO Order 2222 implementation timeline uncertain (2026–2028 at earliest); community must maintain grid connection to participate — does not provide standalone island resilience without additional dedicated storage investment
